trajectory_summary: The call moved in a positive direction — Kelly arrived warm, became engaged, and ended invested enough to book a Friday appointment without resistance. The rep completed the structural beats efficiently. The coaching gap is not that energy leaked; it's that Pike never amplified the energy Kelly brought. A customer this open, with a friend referral and a self-directed bill pull, was set up for a stronger close than she received — the bill was captured but not flipped, the 'if that's true' credibility hedge was left unresolved, and the button-up was thin.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a booked appointment: Friday the 24th at 3PM, confirmed by both parties. Outcome should be tagged: appointment_booked. Fragility is low — Kelly is self-motivated, single decision-maker, no competing objections raised. The main no-show risk is the thin button-up (no 'is that fair' handshake, no explicit computer/no-driving anchor, no personalized text tieback confirmed).

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up completion — handshake, channel anchor, personalized tieback
primary_coaching_focus.quote: So just look out for that text.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Pike booked a willing customer and then left three required close elements on the table: no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no phone/no-driving/computer anchor, no personalized text tieback. Kelly is self-motivated and low-resistance, which means the no-show risk here is entirely within Pike's control — a complete button-up on this call would have been easy. The manager should walk Pike through the button-up checklist against this specific close and build the habit before Pike faces a harder customer where the incomplete close actually costs a sit.

key_moments.quote: But go ahead, sir. I didn't mean to cut you off.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kelly is female — she introduced herself by name at 00:10 ('Is this Kelly? Yeah. This is Kelly.'). Addressing her as 'sir' is a gender error. On a warm, self-motivated customer this early in the call, it's a micro-fracture rather than a deal-breaker, but it signals Pike was not fully tracking the person on the other end of the line.

key_moments.quote: why would I not do that. Of course. If that's true.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's highest-leverage moment and Pike walked past it. Kelly has already done the cost-benefit math and landed on 'yes' — but she's holding a conditional ('if that's true') that is an open credibility check. The strategic move here is to convert the conditional into a commitment: anchor on the Tesla-certified installer framing from the intro, point to the neighbor solar installations Pike had already noted on the roof view, and let Kelly's own logic close the loop. Instead, Pike moves directly into verify. The enthusiasm signal was real; the amplification was absent.

key_moments.quote: I mean, it depends. Like, in the summer, I'll pay, you know, like, $70. In the winter, it can be closer to $15,200.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kelly gives a bill range with a meaningful winter spike. Pike's response is 'Okay. So it can kinda double in the summer there?' — which misreads the data (Kelly said winter is higher, not summer) and then drops the anchor entirely with 'K. Great job.' The bill was captured as data and immediately abandoned. Per the codex, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The flip opportunity here was direct: Kelly's winter bill is the pain point, and Pike had the number in hand.

key_moments.quote: No. [to credit question] Perfect. And you qualify just fine.
key_moments.stewart_read: Pike asks the credit qualifier at the correct threshold (670, matching the schema) and self-qualifies on Kelly's stated 'no' without any verification. Per qualifiers.credit_qualifier.spencer_operator_note, stated credit ≠ pulled credit, and the policy question of whether setters should DQ on stated score is explicitly unresolved in the codex. Pike's 'you qualify just fine' is a confident declaration based on a self-report. This is not a coaching failure if Ion's policy is setter-DQs-on-stated — but if the policy is closer-pulls-the-credit, Pike has just made a promise the closer may not be able to keep.
